---
title: "Lab 10: Infrastructure as Code & User Authorization"
---

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## readme.txt — Lab Objectives

In Lab 9, we used a sequence of `gcloud` commands to build a digital rockhounding vault. If you deleted your infrastructure (as you should have!), rebuilding it manually would be a chore. In this lab we will:

1.  Use **Terraform** to automatically recreate our entire database, storage bucket, and IAM permissions in seconds.
2.  Update our Rockhound App to support user Authorization.
3.  Use **Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)** to secure the app and allow users to mark their geological finds as either "Public" (visible to everyone) or "Private" (visible only to them).
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 0: Lab 9 Cleanup (Manual Teardown)

Before we use Terraform to build our infrastructure declaratively, we need to make sure your project is a blank slate. If you haven't already torn down your resources from Lab 9, run the following commands to delete them.

::: panel-tabset
## Mac / Linux / Cloud Shell

``` bash
# Delete the App Engine Service
gcloud app services delete cloud-data-lab --quiet

# Delete the Firestore Database
gcloud firestore databases delete --database=geology-vault

# Delete the Bucket (and all images inside)
gcloud storage rm -r gs://[YOUR-PROJECT-ID]-specimen-vault
```

## Windows (PowerShell)

``` powershell
# Delete the App Engine Service
gcloud app services delete cloud-data-lab --quiet

# Delete the Firestore Database
gcloud firestore databases delete --database=geology-vault

# Delete the Bucket (and all images inside)
gcloud storage rm -r gs://[YOUR-PROJECT-ID]-specimen-vault
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 1: The Terraform Refresher

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Declarative vs. Imperative

- In previous labs, we used **Imperative** commands (e.g., `gcloud storage buckets create...`). 
- Imperative means telling the cloud *exactly how* to do something, step by step.
- Recall that Terraform uses a **Declarative** model. 
- You write a configuration file that describes what the final architecture *should* look like, and Terraform figures out how to build it.
- If the bucket already exists, Terraform does nothing. If it doesn't, Terraform creates it. 
- This is the foundation of **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)**.
:::

### 1.1 The Configuration File

- Open your Google Cloud Shell. Create a new directory for this lab, and inside it, create a file named `main.tf`. 
- Paste the following code. Notice how it provisions our APIs, Bucket, Database, and IAM roles all in one file!

**`main.tf`**
```hcl
variable "project_id" {
  description = "Your Google Cloud Project ID"
  type        = string
}

provider "google" {
  project = var.project_id
  region  = "us-west1"
}

# 1. Enable Required APIs
resource "google_project_service" "iam_credentials" {
  service            = "iamcredentials.googleapis.com"
  disable_on_destroy = false
}

resource "google_project_service" "cloud_resource_manager" {
  service            = "cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com"
  disable_on_destroy = false
}

# 2. Provision the Private Bucket
resource "google_storage_bucket" "specimen_vault" {
  name          = "${var.project_id}-specimen-vault"
  location      = "US-WEST1"
  force_destroy = true 
}

# 3. Provision the Named Firestore Database
resource "google_firestore_database" "database" {
  name        = "geology-vault"
  location_id = "nam5"
  type        = "FIRESTORE_NATIVE"
}

# 4. Grant App Engine the ability to Sign URLs and Access Storage
resource "google_project_iam_binding" "token_creator" {
  project = var.project_id
  role    = "roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator"
  members = ["serviceAccount:${var.project_id}@appspot.gserviceaccount.com"]

  # Tell Terraform it MUST wait for this API to be enabled before assigning the role
  depends_on = [google_project_service.cloud_resource_manager]
}

resource "google_storage_bucket_iam_binding" "bucket_admin" {
  bucket = google_storage_bucket.specimen_vault.name
  role   = "roles/storage.objectAdmin"
  members = ["serviceAccount:${var.project_id}@appspot.gserviceaccount.com"]
}
```

### 1.2 Deploying the Infrastructure

In your Cloud Shell terminal, we need to run a sequence of commands to execute your script. 

::: {.callout-warning collapse="false"}
## The "Chicken and Egg" Bootstrapping Problem
- Terraform needs to read your project's current state before it builds anything. 
- To do this, it relies on Google's **Cloud Resource Manager API**. However, in a Zero-Trust cloud, that API is turned off by default! 
- If we try to use Terraform to turn it on, Terraform will crash before it even starts because it doesn't have permission to look at the project yet. Therefore, we must manually "bootstrap" the project by enabling this single API using `gcloud` before we hand the reins over to Terraform.
:::

Run the following commands in order:

```bash
# 1. Bootstrap the project by manually opening the Resource Manager door
gcloud services enable cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com

# 2. Set your project ID as an environment variable for Terraform
export TF_VAR_project_id=$(gcloud config get-value project)

# 3. Initialize Terraform (downloads the Google Cloud plugins)
terraform init

# 4. Apply the configuration to build the architecture
terraform apply -auto-approve
```
*Wait about 2-3 minutes for the script to finish building your environment.*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 2: The Multi-User "Field Notes" App

Now that our infrastructure is back online, we need to update our Python application.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## How IAP Passes Identity to Code

- When we turn on **Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)** in Part 3, IAP acts as a bouncer. 
- If a user successfully logs in, IAP forwards their traffic to our App Engine code and secretly attaches a special HTTP header: ```X-Goog-Authenticated-User-Email```
- It also attaches a cryptographically signed **JSON Web Token (JWT)** that proves the email header wasn't forged by a hacker.
- We will use this data inside our Python code to tag who owns which rock, filter the database, and display the raw JWT token on the screen!
:::

### 2.1 The Application Files

Create the `requirements.txt`, `app.yaml`, and `main.py` files in the same directory as your Terraform file.

**`requirements.txt`**
```text
Flask==3.0.0
google-cloud-storage==2.14.0
google-cloud-firestore==2.14.0
google-auth==2.23.3
requests==2.31.0
```

**`app.yaml`**
```yaml
runtime: python311
service: cloud-data-lab
automatic_scaling:
  max_instances: 1
```

**`main.py`**
```python
import os, uuid
from flask import Flask, render_template_string, request, redirect
from google.cloud import storage, firestore
from google.cloud.firestore_v1.base_query import FieldFilter
import google.auth
import google.auth.transport.requests

app = Flask(__name__)
PROJECT_ID = os.environ.get('GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT')
BUCKET_NAME = f"{PROJECT_ID}-specimen-vault"

db = firestore.Client(database="geology-vault")
storage_client = storage.Client()

def get_current_user():
    """Extracts the logged-in user's email provided by Identity-Aware Proxy."""
    # IAP prepends 'accounts.google.com:' to the email, so we strip it out
    email = request.headers.get('X-Goog-Authenticated-User-Email', '')
    return email.replace('accounts.google.com:', '') if email else 'Anonymous'

def get_raw_jwt():
    """Extracts the raw JSON Web Token (JWT) sent by Identity-Aware Proxy."""
    return request.headers.get('X-Goog-Iap-Jwt-Assertion', 'No Token Found (IAP may be off)')

@app.route('/')
def index():
    current_user = get_current_user()
    jwt_token = get_raw_jwt()
    
    # AuthZ: Query 1 - Get ONLY the community's public finds
    public_docs = db.collection("specimens").where(filter=FieldFilter("is_public", "==", True)).stream()
    public_specimens = [{"id": doc.id, **doc.to_dict()} for doc in public_docs]
    
    # AuthZ: Query 2 - Get ONLY the current user's private finds
    private_docs = db.collection("specimens").where(filter=FieldFilter("owner_email", "==", current_user)).where(filter=FieldFilter("is_public", "==", False)).stream()
    private_specimens = [{"id": doc.id, **doc.to_dict()} for doc in private_docs]
    
    html = """
    <div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;">
        <h1>Rockhound Vault</h1>
        <a href="/_gcp_iap/clear_login_cookie" style="padding: 8px 16px; background-color: #d93025; color: white; text-decoration: none; border-radius: 4px;">Log Out</a>
    </div>
    <p>Logged in as: <strong>{{ user }}</strong></p>
    <hr>
    
    <h3>🌍 Public Community Finds</h3>
    <ul>
    {% for s in public_specimens %}
        <li><strong>{{ s.mineral_type }}</strong> (Found by: {{ s.owner_email }}) - <a href="/specimen/{{ s.id }}">View</a></li>
    {% else %}
        <li><em>No public specimens yet.</em></li>
    {% endfor %}
    </ul>

    <h3>🔒 My Private Vault</h3>
    <ul>
    {% for s in private_specimens %}
        <li><strong>{{ s.mineral_type }}</strong> (Found in: {{ s.location.county }} County) - <a href="/specimen/{{ s.id }}">View</a></li>
    {% else %}
        <li><em>You have no private logs.</em></li>
    {% endfor %}
    </ul>
    
    <hr>
    <h3>Log New Specimen</h3>
    <form action="/add" method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data">
        <input type="text" name="mineral_type" placeholder="Mineral (e.g., Beryl)" required><br><br>
        <input type="text" name="county" placeholder="County (e.g., Missoula)" required><br><br>
        <textarea name="notes" placeholder="Field notes..."></textarea><br><br>
        <label>Upload Photo (Private GCS):</label><br>
        <input type="file" name="image" accept="image/*" required><br><br>
        
        <input type="checkbox" name="is_public" id="is_public">
        <label for="is_public"><strong>Make this find Public</strong> (Everyone can see it)</label><br><br>
        
        <button type="submit">Upload to Vault</button>
    </form>

    <hr>
    <div style="background-color: #f1f3f4; padding: 15px; border-radius: 5px; font-family: monospace; word-break: break-all; margin-top: 40px;">
        <h4 style="margin-top: 0; color: #1a73e8;">Diagnostic: Raw IAP JWT Assertion</h4>
        <p style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555;">This is the cryptographically signed token Google passed to your Python code behind the scenes!</p>
        {{ token }}
    </div>
    """
    return render_template_string(html, user=current_user, public_specimens=public_specimens, private_specimens=private_specimens, token=jwt_token)

@app.route('/add', methods=['POST'])
def add_specimen():
    current_user = get_current_user()
    mineral = request.form.get('mineral_type')
    county = request.form.get('county')
    notes = request.form.get('notes')
    is_public = 'is_public' in request.form # Returns True if checkbox was checked
    image_file = request.files.get('image')
    
    filename = "default.jpg"
    if image_file and image_file.filename != '':
        filename = f"{uuid.uuid4().hex}.{image_file.filename.split('.')[-1]}"
        storage_client.bucket(BUCKET_NAME).blob(filename).upload_from_file(image_file)
        
    doc_ref = db.collection("specimens").document()
    doc_ref.set({
        "mineral_type": mineral,
        "notes": notes,
        "image_path": filename,
        "owner_email": current_user, # Save who created this!
        "is_public": is_public,      # Save the privacy status!
        "location": {"county": county, "lat": 0.0, "lng": 0.0}
    })
    
    return redirect('/')

@app.route('/specimen/<doc_id>')
def view_specimen(doc_id):
    doc = db.collection("specimens").document(doc_id).get()
    if not doc.exists:
        return "Specimen not found", 404
        
    data = doc.to_dict()
    current_user = get_current_user()
    
    # SECURITY ENFORCEMENT: If it's private, and the current user doesn't own it, BLOCK ACCESS!
    if not data.get('is_public') and data.get('owner_email') != current_user:
        return "<h1>HTTP 403: Forbidden</h1><p>You do not have permission to view this private log.</p>", 403

    blob = storage_client.bucket(BUCKET_NAME).blob(data.get('image_path', 'default.jpg'))
    credentials, _ = google.auth.default()
    credentials.refresh(google.auth.transport.requests.Request())
    
    img_url = blob.generate_signed_url(
        version="v4", expiration=900, 
        service_account_email=credentials.service_account_email or f"{PROJECT_ID}@appspot.gserviceaccount.com",
        access_token=credentials.token
    ) if blob.exists() else ""

    html = """
    <h1>{{ data.mineral_type }} {% if not data.is_public %} 🔒 {% endif %}</h1>
    <p><strong>Logged by:</strong> {{ data.owner_email }}</p>
    <p><strong>County:</strong> {{ data.location.county }}</p>
    <p><strong>Notes:</strong> {{ data.notes }}</p>
    {% if img_url %}<img src="{{ img_url }}" width="400" style="border: 3px solid #333;">{% endif %}
    <br><br><a href="/">Back to Vault</a>
    """
    return render_template_string(html, data=data, img_url=img_url)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=8080)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.2 Establish the OAuth Consent Screen & Credentials

Before Google allows you to ask people to log in, you need to configure a consent screen and manually create an OAuth client ID. This two-step process gives IAP the credentials it needs to function.

**Step A: Configure the OAuth Consent Screen**

1.  Use the top search bar in the Google Cloud Console to search for **OAuth consent screen** (under APIs & Services) and click it.
2.  Choose **External** (or **Internal** if prompted by a university Workspace account) and click **Create**.
3.  Fill out the required **App Name** and **Support Email** fields, then click **Save and Continue**.
4.  On the **Scopes** page, click **Save and Continue** (no changes needed).
5.  On the **Test Users** page, click **Save and Continue** (no changes needed).
6.  On the **Summary** page, click **Back to Dashboard**.

**Step B: Manually Create the OAuth Client ID**

1.  In the left sidebar, navigate to **APIs & Services → Credentials**.
2.  Click **+ Create Credentials** and choose **OAuth client ID**.
3.  For Application type, select **Web application**.
4.  Give it a name (e.g., `IAP-App-Engine`) and click **Create**.
5.  A dialog will show your new Client ID and Secret — click **OK** to dismiss it.

IAP will automatically detect and use this credential when you enable it in the next step.
------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 4: Testing & Teardown

### 4.1 Test the Authorization Logic

Visit your App Engine URL. 

1.  You should be forced to log in with Google.
2.  Your email should be displayed at the top of the dashboard.
3.  Scroll to the bottom of the page to view the **Raw IAP JWT Assertion**. This is the literal cryptographically signed ID badge you learned about in the lecture!
4.  Upload one **Private** specimen. Notice that it only appears under "My Private Vault".
5.  Upload one **Public** specimen. Notice that it appears under the "Public Community Finds".
6.  Click the red **Log Out** button at the top right.
7.  *(Optional)* If you granted access to a classmate, have them log in. They should see your Public find, but your Private find will be completely invisible to them!

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Lab Submission Requirements

To receive full credit for Lab 10, submit a single PDF document containing:

1.  **The Live URL:** Provide the direct link to your deployed `cloud-data-lab` App Engine service.
2.  **The AuthN Screenshot:** A screenshot of the Google Login prompt before entering your app, proving IAP is active.
3.  **The AuthZ Screenshot:** A screenshot of your dashboard showing at least one "Public" find, one "Private" find, and the raw JWT Diagnostic box at the bottom.
:::

### 4.2 Infrastructure Teardown

To practice destroying infrastructure via code, we can use Terraform to delete everything we just built! Because Terraform kept a detailed receipt in the `terraform.tfstate` file, it knows exactly what to destroy in the correct order.

In Cloud Shell, run:
```bash
terraform destroy -auto-approve
```

::: {.callout-warning collapse="false"}
## Manual Cleanup Reminder

Terraform will delete the bucket and IAM policies, but Google Cloud puts Firestore in a "locked" state by default to prevent accidental data deletion. You may still need to delete the `geology-vault` database manually from the Cloud Console, and run `gcloud app services delete cloud-data-lab --quiet` to clean up App Engine.
:::
